turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Good morning, this is The Morning Commute. Tuesday, June twenty-third. I'm Sam.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: And I'm Alex. The throughline today is the Iran de-escalation working its way through every rates and FX market on the board — plus an AI power-demand story that's quietly the biggest capex theme of the decade. Let's get into it.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Headlines. World desk first. Starmer.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Starmer's stepping down as Labour leader, staying on as caretaker PM through the contest. Burnham's the bookies' favorite, running from outside the parliamentary party — which is the interesting part, because he'd need a seat.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: The market question is fiscal continuity. Does a Burnham-led Labour reopen the spending envelope Reeves spent two years defending?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's exactly the tail risk gilts will price. The front end is fine — it's the long end that gets nervous if the market sniffs a looser fiscal stance. Watch the thirty-year and the sterling basis. For now it's a slow-burn re-rating, not a Truss-style gap move, because there's no fiscal event attached yet.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So a fade, not a break. Second story — Iran.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The June eighteenth MOU is holding. Hormuz reopening, the US lifting the naval blockade, and Bessent's sixty-day general license greenlighting Iranian barrels through August. The war kicked off late February, took Brent to the low eighties and US retail gas to four-fifty-six at the Hormuz peak.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: But the curve already de-risked most of that. How much war premium is actually left to bleed out?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Less than people think — that's the point. The spot move is mostly done; the action now is in the term structure. The back end was pricing a structural supply impairment that's no longer the base case, so you're seeing the curve flatten as the geopolitical premium comes out of the deferreds. The real swing factor is how fast Iranian volumes actually ramp, and whether OPEC-plus accommodates or defends price.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the snapback risk if the MOU breaks?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's the asymmetry — it's a sixty-day license, not a treaty. Optionality is cheap here for a reason. But the directional read is disinflationary, and that's what's driving the rates story we're about to get into.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Perfect bridge. Market overview.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Monday's close: S&P off thirty-seven basis points to seventy-four seventy-three, Nasdaq down one-point-three to twenty-six-one-sixty-six, Dow up a quarter percent. Classic breadth divergence — it was a megacap problem, not a market problem.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: The tell is the Russell holding while the Nasdaq bled. That's de-grossing in the crowded book, not risk-off.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Right. Equal-weight outperformed cap-weight by a wide margin, advancers beat decliners — under the hood it was actually a decent tape. The index headline is just Mag-Seven concentration doing what concentration does.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the catalyst — Alphabet down five on AI talent departures. Is that a real earnings signal or a positioning excuse?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Mostly a positioning excuse dressed as a narrative. When a name is that consensus-long and richly multiplied, you don't need much — a couple of star researchers walking to a competitor reprices the terminal-value story at the margin. Amazon down almost five, Microsoft off three, Meta down a couple. SpaceX was the real carnage, down sixteen, third straight session — that one's a genuine de-rating, not noise.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Where does that leave the Fed setup into Warsh?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: This is the crux. Warsh is the incoming chair, and his reaction function explicitly discounts supply-side energy shocks — he'd look straight through the oil spike. So with crude now reversing, he's got cover to cut into a market that's already pricing easing. The hawkish objection is the independence question — a Trump pick cutting into a still-warm core — but the data's cooperating.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And earnings give him air cover.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Twenty-two percent Q2 S&P earnings growth on the FactSet blend, second straight quarter north of twenty. You can tolerate a lot of macro noise when the E in the multiple is compounding like that. Net: constructive, with the caveat that the megacap complex just reminded everyone the beta works both ways.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: US business. Two deals.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Roku-Fox, merger agreement signed the fourteenth. The strategic logic is connected-TV ad inventory plus automatic content recognition data — Roku owns the OS layer and the home screen, Fox brings live sports, news, and Tubi's FAST inventory.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So this is a play on the same CTV ad pool The Trade Desk and Amazon are fighting over. The question is whether owning content plus the OS is vertical integration regulators tolerate.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's the whole antitrust question. Roku monetizes the screen regardless of what you watch; bolt on Fox's content and Tubi's reach and you've got a vertically integrated CTV ad machine. Expect a long second request, and watch how they argue the ad market is still fragmented enough to clear it.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Deal two — Olin and Huntsman.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Merger of equals, twelve-billion-plus North American chemicals platform. Plus Ingredion taking Tate and Lyle for three-point-six. The chemicals tie-up is a classic late-cycle defensive consolidation — chlor-alkali and epoxy have been grinding through a trough with Chinese oversupply capping any pricing recovery.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So synergy math over a growth thesis. Is the combined leverage profile the risk, or the antitrust overlap in epoxies?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Both get scrutiny, but it's the synergy credibility that makes or breaks the multiple — these are commodity cycles, and the street will haircut the cost-out number until they see it. The broader signal is the one that matters: when organic growth is scarce, boards buy it, and the M&A pipeline keeps building.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Two international.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The G10 central bank divergence is the trade. ECB's expected to deliver an insurance hike to lean against the energy pass-through into core, and the BOJ's tilting toward another step in normalization. So you've got Europe and Japan tightening into a Fed that wants to cut.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: That's straight rate-differential fuel for euro and yen. Does it finally break the dollar smile, or does it just compress the carry?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It compresses the carry first — and the more interesting leg is the yen, because a hawkish BOJ plus a dovish Fed is exactly the setup that unwinds yen-funded carry. That's the cross-asset risk nobody's hedged for: a disorderly carry unwind doesn't stay in FX, it shows up in everything that got funded cheaply.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Second story.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Japan again — the structural one. The megabanks are dropping their long-standing refusal to finance defense, with the policy banks already easing terms. And JAXA put up the H3 number-six on a low-cost configuration, restoring sovereign launch and going after commercial share.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Defense plus space — same thesis as the German fiscal turn. Re-armament as a multi-year capex cycle the market's still under-positioned for.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Exactly the read. This is balance-sheet capital reallocating toward security as a secular theme, not a headline trade. Money follows geopolitics, and Japan's the cleanest expression of it this cycle.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: China to close the block.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Two-speed, and the speeds are diverging. Beijing set four-and-a-half to five on growth, PBoC signaling no more cuts this year — they're guarding the rate differential and the currency. Exports up fifteen percent in the first four months, investment firm; consumption soft, property still a drag.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: But the nominal picture is worse than the real one — the GDP deflator's been negative. Isn't the actual story debt-deflation, not the headline growth print?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's the right lens. Real growth near target masks nominal growth that's barely positive, because the deflator's underwater — PPI's been in deflation for a long stretch. That's the trap: nominal debt service against falling nominal incomes, with LGFV liabilities and property both compounding it. The export strength is partly tariff front-running, which borrows from future quarters.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the one datapoint that captures the model shift.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: BYD past Tesla as the world's largest EV seller by volume. China's not competing on cost anymore — it's competing on the car. The catch is the domestic price war is torching margins, which is the overcapacity problem in miniature.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: That's the block. Changing gears.

segments.raw: SEGMENT TWO — ENERGY / DATA CENTERS / UTILITIES
segments.label: Energy / Data Centers / Utilities

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Segment two — the energy desk. The AI-power collision. Alex.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The June marker is DOE standing up Agora — a simulation platform to model how hyperscale campuses behave on the grid. The reason it matters: these aren't conventional loads. A training cluster swings from near-idle to full draw in seconds, so to the grid it looks like a multi-hundred-megawatt industrial load slamming on instantly.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And that volatility is a frequency-stability problem, not just a capacity problem. Steady demand you can plan; sub-second ramps you have to engineer around.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Which is the whole reliability headache. PJM is projecting a six-gigawatt shortfall against its reliability requirement in twenty twenty-seven — the market monitor's words were "crisis stage." And the demand curve is eighty gigawatts in twenty twenty-five to a hundred-fifty by twenty-eight. That's a Spain-sized load added in three years.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: That's why the last PJM capacity auction cleared at records. The interconnection queue is the binding constraint — you can announce gigawatts of campuses, but you can't get the steel and the transformers and the queue position to match.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Queue, transformers, and gas turbines — the OEM backlogs run years out now. That's why the build is going behind-the-meter: hyperscalers signing dedicated generation, the nuclear restart deals, the SMR pre-orders that won't deliver this decade. None of it closes a twenty twenty-seven gap, which means gas and keeping retiring coal online longer.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the cost allocation fight — who eats the grid upgrades. That's the political fuse.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's where it turns from a capex story into a ballot-box story. When ratepayers see transmission upgrades for hyperscaler load showing up in their bills, the cross-subsidization question gets political fast. The investable surface: regulated utilities with the rate base, the IPPs with merchant exposure to rising power prices, the gas-turbine OEMs, and the nuclear complex. Multi-year theme, and it touches inflation through the power bill.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: There's a water angle too — evaporative cooling against drought-region siting.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Millions of gallons a day per campus, and it collides with exactly the Sun-Belt geographies where the cheap land and the interconnect are. Power, water, and community opposition are converging into the real constraint on the buildout — arguably tighter than chips at this point.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Bring it home.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Segment three — the Philippines. Weather lead, then the macro.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Super Typhoon Francisco — local name Mekkhala — holding super-typhoon strength, Signal Number One up across northern Luzon. The economic wrinkle: that's the rice belt, so a hard hit feeds the food-inflation print at precisely the wrong moment.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Because the BSP's already boxed in. Walk me through the policy bind.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: They flipped from easing to hiking — after two-and-a-quarter points of cuts since August twenty twenty-four, they took twenty-five basis points back in April to four-and-a-half, first hike in two years, and flagged more small moves. The driver is imported inflation — oil and fertilizer pushing the projection above the four-percent ceiling for both twenty twenty-six and twenty-seven.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: So they're hiking into a currency problem as much as an inflation problem. The peso past sixty-point-seven is the real forcing function, isn't it?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: It is — near a record low, and the BSP can't let the pass-through from a weak peso get disorderly. It's broad dollar strength plus the oil-import bill widening the external position. The silver lining is the one we opened with: the Iran de-escalation pulling crude down takes pressure off both the inflation path and the currency simultaneously. That's the cleanest macro tailwind they've had in months.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the structural offsets — remittances and BPO.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Remittances around a tenth of GDP, and a weaker peso actually flatters the converted value, so it's a stabilizer for household consumption even as imports bite. BPO is the dollar earner to watch — and the AI overhang we just discussed is a genuine medium-term risk to the lower end of that stack. Growth's still seen recovering to about five-one this year from four-four. So: cyclically pressured, structurally intact.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Quick local color.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Batac in Ilocos Norte marks its nineteenth charter anniversary today; Tabuk in Kalinga's got a founding-anniversary holiday — part of a long list the palace declared across nineteen areas this month.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Onward.

segments.raw: SEGMENT FOUR — VOCAB OF THE DAY
segments.label: Vocab Of The Day

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Segment four — vocab. Two Mandarin, two Tagalog. Mandarin first, and slow it down — I'm actually drilling these.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Word one ties to the Iran story: negotiation. tán pàn. First syllable rises — tán — like a question. Second falls hard — pàn — like setting your foot down. Together, tán-pàn.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: tán-pàn.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Full sentence: liǎng guó zhèng zài jìn xíng tán pàn — "the two countries are negotiating." The chunk to keep is zhèng zài tán pàn — "currently negotiating."

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: zhèng zài tán pàn. Formal register, or everyday?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Newsroom and boardroom register. For haggling a price you'd reach for tǎo jià huán jià. Say the chunk once more.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: zhèng zài tán pàn. Got it.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Word two ties to the energy block: power grid. diàn wǎng. diàn falls — fourth tone — and it's electricity, the same diàn in diàn nǎo, "electric brain," computer. wǎng dips and rises — third tone — net or web, same wǎng as the internet. diàn wǎng — electric web. The grid.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: diàn wǎng. Electric web. That image makes it stick.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Sentence, folding in today's story: shù jù zhōng xīn gěi diàn wǎng dài lái hěn dà yā lì — "data centers put enormous pressure on the power grid."

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Quick tone check before we leave Mandarin — I keep collapsing the falling and the dipping.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Drill the contrast directly: pàn is fourth tone, falling, decisive — pàn. wǎng is third tone, dipping then rising — wǎng. Back to back: pàn, wǎng. That one contrast carries most of your error rate.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: pàn... wǎng. Falling, dipping. Clean.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Now Tagalog — and for native speakers, so we go for nuance, not glossary. Word one: alimpuyo. ah-leem-POO-yoh. A whirl, a vortex — of wind, water, or feeling. Alimpuyo ng hangin, whirlwind; alimpuyo ng damdamin, turmoil of emotion.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: The native-speaker distinction from ipo-ipo?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Weight and register. Ipo-ipo is the small, almost playful whirlwind in the yard. Alimpuyo is literary, forceful, churning — the word for a super typhoon's fury, or a chaotic market session. In a sentence: sa gitna ng alimpuyo ng merkado, kailangan ng malamig na ulo — "in the middle of the market's turbulence, keep a cool head."

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And word two.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: sapantaha. sah-pahn-TAH-hah. A conjecture, a reasoned supposition — a view formed before the facts are in. Against haka-haka, which leans toward idle, unfounded speculation, sapantaha is dignified — a considered hypothesis. Sapantaha lang ito, ngunit malamang tama — "this is only a conjecture, but it's likely correct."

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: One a hunch, the other a thesis. And both live in today's show — the alimpuyo of the typhoon and the tape, the sapantaha behind every rate-cut bet.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Language inside the news. That's the idea.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Segment five — art and pop, quick.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Rock revival — The Strokes drop their seventh, "Reality Awaits," the twenty-sixth, reportedly back to the early sound. And Charli XCX, off scoring the new "Wuthering Heights," announced "Music, Fashion, Film" for late July.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Album-of-the-year conversation?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: BTS's "Arirang" comeback is the headline, leaning into Korean roots — plus strong returns from Harry Styles, Robyn, Noah Kahan, Olivia Rodrigo. July's stacked: Madonna, Ariana Grande, Tyla all landing.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the screen side?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The telling one — "Mandalorian and Grogu" underperforming the usual Star Wars curve, to the point the Backrooms film, born from an internet horror meme, out-earned it. Cultural energy migrating from legacy IP to internet-native stories. The slow-burn structural story is the Taylor Swift–Disney tie-up reshaping how one catalog drives a whole ecosystem.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Internet-native culture eating the old guard. Good cue for the last segment.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Segment six — the meme.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The format eating feeds is the "supportive, disappointed, sarcastic, flirty" challenge — one line delivered four ways, the whole gag in how far the meaning bends on tone alone. It's everywhere because it's endlessly remixable, and it's the rare format that works without sound.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And the meta-trend behind it?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: The "Great Meme Reset of twenty twenty-six" — teenagers organizing to ditch AI brain-rot and bring back the classics. The kids unionizing against the algorithm. Also live: "New York is Gotham" on every gritty subway clip, and the Spurs–Knicks Finals eating the timeline meme by meme.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Rooting for the kids.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: And before we let you drive — one good thing to end on.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: A genuinely hopeful one. A major global study out this month finds mangrove forests are now gaining faster than they're being lost — the first time that trend has flipped in sixteen years, as restoration projects and tougher protections finally start to bite. And the reason it lands today: mangroves are the cheapest storm barrier coastal communities have. After our typhoon block, that one's close to home.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: Nature quietly winning one. Anything else on the good-news wire?

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: New Zealand's kākāpō — the flightless night parrot, down to two hundred thirty-five adults — just posted its best breeding season on record: over a hundred chicks this year, against an old high of eighty-five. For one of the rarest birds on earth, that's an enormous margin.

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: A rebounding coastline and a hundred new parrots. We'll take it.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: That's your commute. Whether it's an alimpuyo out there or smooth sailing —

turns.speaker: SAM
turns.text: — pace yourself, hydrate, back here tomorrow.

turns.speaker: ALEX
turns.text: Drive safe.
